content:
Unsupervised structure discovery predates the term "machine learning." Early **statistics** (factor analysis, principal components, multidimensional scaling) sought low-dimensional explanations of correlated measurements without prediction targets.

**Cluster analysis** grew in anthropology and biology (taxonomy, morphometrics) as a way to organize specimens by measured traits when no authoritative class labels existed.

The phrase **unsupervised learning** became standard in the neural-network and pattern-recognition literature of the 1980s–1990s alongside **supervised** and **reinforcement** paradigms—contrasting label-free representation learning (autoencoders, competitive learning) with classification.

Modern data science pipelines inherit the same split: explore with unsupervised summaries and segments, then invest in labels for supervised prediction where ROI is clear.

timeline.label: Factor analysis and PCA
timeline.description: Early multivariate methods extract latent factors and principal directions from unlabeled measurements.

timeline.label: Cluster analysis in taxonomy
timeline.description: Hierarchical and partitioning cluster methods organize objects by similarity without predefined classes.

timeline.label: Neural unsupervised learning
timeline.description: Autoencoders, SOMs, and competitive learning popularize learning representations without label supervision.

timeline.label: Modern EDA and representation pre-training
timeline.description: Unsupervised embeddings and segmentation underpin exploration, monitoring, and pre-training before fine-tuning.
